items.title: GhostTree: Unveiling Path Manipulation Techniques to Bypass Windows Security
items.summary: Most security teams think of NTFS junctions and symbolic links as niche file system features. They let one directory point to another, like a shortcut that the OS treats as real. They exist for backward compatibility, storage management, things that rarely come up in a SOC. But they have a property that makes them interesting from an offensive perspective: any user can create them. No admin privileges are required, and no special permissions beyond write access to the target folder.

items.title: Re: On the issue of MIME handlers that execute arbitrary code (e.g. Wine)
items.summary:
Posted by Aaron Rainbolt on May 19 Someone in the Wine bug report mentioned portability as a concern with 
getting Wine to handle EXE files transparently. binfmt-misc can be used 
on Linux, but not on the BSDs, which Wine also supports. I'm not sure 
there is any good mechanism on BSD to mark a file type as executable, 
which is ultimately what Wine is trying to do. MIME handlers are a 
(bad, but possibly the only portable) way to work around that 
limitation.

items.title: Swedish fugitive arrested as new OTF GRIMM targets added to EU Most Wanted
items.summary: The arrested individual, a man in his 30s linked to the Foxtrot network, was wanted by Swedish authorities for murder, preparation, and conspiracy to commit murder. He was suspected of playing an active role within an organised crime group, including the recruitment of individuals to carry out violent acts. items.title: EU Most Wanted platform helps locate Hungarian child abuser hiding in Spain
items.summary: The 37-year-old man was arrested in Tenerife on 7 May, three days after his profile was published on the platform. items.title: “Terrorgram” network dealt another blow as member sentenced in Denmark
items.summary: A Danish-French national has been sentenced to six years in prison for terrorism-related offences by the District Court in Glostrup, following an investigation supported by Europol’s European Counter Terrorism Centre (ECTC). A Europol specialist was called to testify during the court proceedings. items.title: Call centres dismantled and ten arrested in EUR 50 million online fraud case
items.summary: A criminal network operating a large-scale online fraud scheme has been dismantled through a collaborative investigation involving Austrian and Albanian authorities, with support from Europol and Eurojust. The operation, which spanned over two years, resulted in the arrest of ten individuals, the search of multiple premises, and the seizure of nearly EUR 900 000 in cash.

items.title: OTF GRIMM one-year anniversary: violence-as-a-service targets published on EU Most Wanted
items.summary: As the Operational Taskforce (OTF) GRIMM marks its first year of activity, high-value targets linked to ongoing investigations are being published on the EU Most Wanted website. The publication aims to intensify efforts to locate and arrest some of the most dangerous fugitives involved in violence-as-a-service and other forms of serious organised crime.

items.title: Europol supports hit against ‘Black Axe’ criminal organisation in Switzerland: 10 arrests
items.summary: Europol has supported a comprehensive investigation conducted by Swiss authorities in collaboration with German law enforcement against members of the international criminal organisation ‘Black Axe’. On 28 April 2026, house searches were carried out in several Swiss cantons, resulting in a total of 10 arrests, including the Black Axe “Regional Head” for the Southern European region.
